content.text: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and Serverless are not competing products — they are points on a spectrum of how much infrastructure you manage versus how much the cloud vendor manages for you. The decision you make determines your operational overhead, deployment speed, cost model, and the ceiling on customization.

Choosing incorrectly in either direction is expensive: picking IaaS when PaaS would suffice means you spend engineering time managing servers instead of building product. Picking SaaS when you need custom logic means you hit walls you cannot break through.
Each model shifts a different number of layers from your team to the vendor. The diagram below maps every infrastructure layer and shows who is responsible in each model.

attrs.alt: Shared responsibility stack: IaaS (you manage app through OS), PaaS (you manage app only), Serverless/FaaS (you manage function code only), SaaS (vendor manages everything). Yellow = your responsibility, teal = vendor responsibility.
attrs.title: IaaS vs PaaS vs Serverless vs SaaS — Shared Responsibility

content.text: Reading the chart: as you move from IaaS to SaaS, the yellow (your responsibility) rows shrink and the teal (vendor responsibility) rows grow. Your operational burden decreases but so does your ability to customize.
App Service, Azure SQL, AKS, Cosmos DB
Azure Functions, Container Apps, Logic Apps
Medium — limited by execution time, runtime
Low — deploy code, handle cold starts
Legacy lift-and-shift, GPU workloads, custom OS
Commodity tools: email, CRM, source control
Deep Dive: Control vs Convenience Trade-off

With IaaS you choose the VM SKU, install the OS and runtime, configure networking and security groups, mount and format disks, and maintain everything. This level of control is irreplaceable when you need:
A specific OS configuration that a managed runtime cannot provide (custom kernel modules, licensed software requiring BIOS-level configuration)
GPU access for deep learning training (NVIDIA A100, H100 VMs on Azure NC/ND series)
Lift-and-shift of on-premises applications where rewriting for PaaS is not feasible in the near term
Network topology control — custom routing tables, NVAs, hub-spoke VNet designs

The cost is real: you need engineers who understand OS hardening, patch management, capacity planning, and backup strategy. Unpatched VMs are the most common attack vector in enterprise Azure environments.
PaaS — Managed runtime, your application logic

PaaS services like App Service, Azure SQL, and Azure Kubernetes Service handle the OS, runtime patching, HA, and backup. You ship application code and configuration. This is the default choice for greenfield web applications and APIs.
Key indicators that PaaS is the right fit:
Your team's expertise is in the application layer, not systems administration
You want deployment slots, auto-scale, and integrated certificate management
Your workload is a long-running process (web server, background worker) rather than event-triggered short bursts
Serverless is not simply "cheaper PaaS." It is a fundamentally different execution model: stateless, event-triggered, duration-constrained. It excels when:
Traffic is unpredictable or spiky — you pay nothing when idle, and the platform absorbs traffic spikes automatically
Work is naturally event-driven (queue message, blob upload, scheduled job, webhook)
Each unit of work completes quickly (under 10 minutes on most hosting plans)
It is a poor fit for long-running processes, workloads needing persistent in-process state, or latency-sensitive APIs where cold starts are unacceptable.

SaaS is appropriate when the problem is a solved commodity — email, source control, CRM, BI dashboards. Building any of these from scratch would take years and deliver an inferior product compared to what specialist vendors have built over decades. The engineering principle is simple:
do not build what you can buy, do not buy what you can configure.
Real architectures mix all four models. A typical e-commerce platform on Azure might use:
— App Service hosts the web storefront and REST API
— Azure SQL stores orders and inventory
— Azure Functions process payment webhook events, resize uploaded product images, and send dispatch emails
— A GPU VM cluster runs nightly ML recommendation model training (specific SKU, custom CUDA configuration)
— Microsoft 365 is used for internal email and Teams; GitHub hosts source code and CI/CD; Power BI dashboards report on sales data
Decision Framework: Choosing the Right Model
Work through these questions in order. The first "yes" answer gives you the model to use for that component:
Is this a commodity problem (email, CRM, source control, BI)?
Is the work event-driven, short-duration, and variable-traffic?
Azure Functions scales to zero and costs nothing when idle.
Is it a long-running web app, API, or managed database?
App Service / Azure SQL / Cosmos DB.
Does it need OS-level control, specific hardware (GPU, FPGA), or can't be refactored?
Cost structures differ fundamentally across models. Understanding them prevents bill shock.

A Standard_D4s_v5 VM (4 vCPU, 16 GB) in East US costs approximately $0.192/hour — $138/month whether it serves traffic or not. To reduce cost during off-hours you must either stop the VM (which causes a cold start when restarted) or use VM Scale Sets with a minimum of 0 instances (which then behaves like Serverless but with longer cold start).

App Service charges per plan, not per app. You can host multiple applications on one plan at no additional cost. A P2v3 plan (4 vCPU, 16 GB) costs ~$219/month and can host as many web apps as it can serve. Scaling out adds instances at the same per-instance rate.

The Azure Functions Consumption plan includes 1 million free executions and 400,000 GB-seconds per month. Beyond that: $0.20 per million executions and $0.000016 per GB-second. A function processing 5 million requests/month at 256 MB, 200 ms each costs approximately:
The same workload on an App Service B1 plan ($13.14/month) would cost 16× more at this volume. Serverless wins at low-to-moderate intermittent traffic; PaaS wins at high sustained throughput.

Microsoft 365 Business Premium is ~$22/user/month. For a 100-person company that is $2,200/month — regardless of how much email or Teams usage occurs. This is OpEx-friendly but can become expensive at scale. At 10,000 users, Microsoft 365 E3 ($36/user/month) costs $360,000/month — at which point a custom-built platform might be justified.
Organizations frequently start with one model and migrate as requirements evolve.
Reduce ops burden, enable auto-scale, remove OS management
Medium — app changes if using OS-specific dependencies
Move event handlers, scheduled jobs, and async tasks to Functions
Low — extract specific handlers to Functions
Cloud adoption first step; no app changes needed
Low — use Azure Migrate to replicate VMs
Abandon self-hosted Jira/GitLab in favor of GitHub/Azure DevOps
Common Mistakes When Choosing a Model

Teams familiar with on-premises infrastructure often replicate the same VM-centric pattern in the cloud. The result is cloud sprawl — hundreds of VMs consuming time and budget that PaaS or Serverless could eliminate.
A web server handling 100 sustained requests per second never scales to zero and incurs higher per-execution billing than a fixed App Service plan.
Teams that build a custom email delivery system or source control tool on PaaS spend months re-inventing what Microsoft 365 or GitHub provides, at higher ongoing maintenance cost.

Data leaving Azure (outbound traffic) incurs egress charges regardless of model. An IaaS VM and a PaaS App Service pay the same rate per GB transferred out to the internet. Model choice does not eliminate network billing.

Architecture evolves. Starting on IaaS for a lift-and-shift and then modernizing to PaaS over 12–18 months is a legitimate strategy. Design your code to be cloud-runtime-agnostic (use environment variables, avoid OS-specific APIs) to make future migration easier.
Practical Code Comparison: The Same Task in Each Model

To make the differences concrete, consider a single task: "receive an order, validate it, write it to a database, and send a confirmation email." Here is how the deployment and code structure differ in each model.
IaaS approach — long-running web server on a VM

# Provision a VM (your responsibility: OS, runtime, app server)
az vm create \
  --resource-group rg-orders \
  --name vm-orders \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D2s_v5 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# SSH in and manually install Python, Nginx, your app...
ssh azureuser@<public-ip>
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y python3 python3-pip nginx
pip3 install fastapi uvicorn psycopg2 sendgrid
# ... copy application code, configure systemd, set up cron for log rotation ...
# ... you own: patching, backups, disk expansion, scaling scripts ...
PaaS approach — App Service with managed database

# Create App Service and Azure SQL — no OS to manage
az appservice plan create \
  --name plan-orders \
  --resource-group rg-orders \
  --sku P1v3 --is-linux

az webapp create \
  --name orders-api \
  --resource-group rg-orders \
  --plan plan-orders \
  --runtime "PYTHON:3.11"

az sql server create \
  --name sql-orders \
  --resource-group rg-orders \
  --location eastus

# Deploy: push code via zip or GitHub Actions
az webapp deploy --name orders-api --src-path app.zip --type zip
# Azure handles OS patches, TLS, auto-restart, scale-out

# app.py — FastAPI on App Service; same code runs on any PaaS runtime
from fastapi import FastAPI
import pyodbc, os

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/orders")
async def create_order(order: dict):
    conn_str = os.environ["SQL_CONNECTION_STRING"]  # from app settings
    with pyodbc.connect(conn_str) as conn:
        conn.execute(
            "INSERT INTO orders (id, amount, status) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
            order["id"], order["amount"], "pending"
        )
    # send email via managed identity to Communication Services
    return {"status": "accepted"}
Serverless approach — Azure Functions, triggered by Service Bus

# function_app.py — event-driven; no always-on process
import azure.functions as func
from azure.cosmos import CosmosClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
import json, logging

app = func.FunctionApp()
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()  # module-level: reused across warm invocations
cosmos = CosmosClient("https://myaccount.documents.azure.com", credential)

@app.service_bus_queue_trigger(arg_name="msg", queue_name="orders",
                               connection="SERVICE_BUS_CONNECTION")
def process_order(msg: func.ServiceBusMessage) -> None:
    order = json.loads(msg.get_body().decode())
    logging.info(f"Processing {order['id']}")
    db = cosmos.get_database_client("shop")
    container = db.get_container_client("orders")
    container.upsert_item({**order, "status": "processed"})
    # send confirmation via output binding or direct SDK call

If the order management is a standard e-commerce workflow, a SaaS product like Dynamics 365 Commerce handles it without any code. You configure the product catalogue, payment gateway integration, and order fulfilment workflows through the Dynamics admin portal. The vendor provisions the compute, scales it, and maintains the application. Your team configures business rules, not infrastructure.
When the SaaS product's out-of-the-box workflow is insufficient, you extend it using its plugin model or by calling its API from an Azure Function — combining SaaS and Serverless.
How each model responds to a traffic surge from 10 requests/second to 10,000 requests/second:

Traffic spikes; existing VMs become overloaded; on-call engineer manually adds VMs via the portal; lag of 5–15 minutes. With VM Scale Sets and autoscale rules the lag drops to 2–5 minutes, but you wrote and maintain the rules.

Scale-out rule fires when CPU > 70% for 5 minutes; new instances come online in 1–3 minutes. You configure the rules; Azure provisions the instances. Minimum instance count is 1 (cannot scale to zero).

Traffic spikes; Azure Functions automatically spawns additional instances within seconds (no configuration needed). When traffic drops, instances are reclaimed; at zero traffic, zero instances run. The tradeoff is cold starts on the first request to a new instance.

Scaling is opaque — the vendor handles it. You have no knob to turn. If the vendor's infrastructure is under strain (rare for tier-1 SaaS), you see latency increases; you file a support ticket rather than adding capacity yourself.
Security responsibilities shift alongside operational responsibilities:

One security responsibility never shifts to the vendor: your application code. Regardless of model, SQL injection, insecure deserialization, broken authentication, and other OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities in your code are your responsibility to fix.
What you can observe depends on what you control. The monitoring surface narrows as you move toward SaaS.

Full access — OS metrics (CPU, memory, disk IOPS), network packet capture, process-level profiling, custom kernel probes. You install the Azure Monitor Agent on each VM to stream metrics and logs to Log Analytics. Also use Azure Defender for Servers for threat detection.

App Service integrates with Application Insights (request traces, dependencies, exceptions, live metrics). Platform metrics (HTTP queue length, response time, CPU per instance) are available via Azure Monitor without agent installation. You cannot instrument the OS or runtime internals.
Azure Functions streams invocation logs and metrics to Application Insights automatically when
is set. Per-execution duration, success rate, and cold-start frequency are visible in the Functions Monitor blade. You cannot observe host-level infrastructure — only your function's behavior.

Visibility is limited to what the vendor exposes. Microsoft 365 provides the Microsoft 365 admin center with service health and usage reports. Audit logs go to Microsoft Purview Audit. You can route these to Azure Monitor via the Office 365 Management API for SIEM integration, but you cannot instrument the application itself.
Infrastructure as Code: Provisioning Each Model
All four models can be provisioned via Bicep or Terraform. Here is the key difference in what you declare:

SaaS resources are not provisioned via Bicep — you configure them through the vendor's admin portal or management APIs (Microsoft Graph for Microsoft 365, the Dynamics 365 admin center for Dynamics). Terraform has providers for some SaaS configuration (the AzureAD/Entra provider, the GitHub provider) but not for the application itself.
Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Beyond Compute
Infrastructure compute cost is only one component of TCO. The hidden costs shift across models:
The AZ-900 (Azure Fundamentals) certification tests service model knowledge in the following ways:
"Which model gives you the most control over the OS?" → IaaS
"A company wants to move to the cloud with minimal changes to their existing VMs. Which model?" → IaaS
"Which model allows you to focus only on application code, not infrastructure?" → PaaS
"Microsoft 365 is an example of which model?" → SaaS
"In which model does the user have the fewest infrastructure responsibilities?" → SaaS

Think of the four models as four kinds of restaurant. IaaS is a fully-stocked kitchen — you cook everything from raw ingredients. PaaS is a catered workspace — the kitchen is set up, you just bring your recipe. Serverless is a food delivery service — you place an order, it arrives hot, you pay per meal, and you never see the kitchen. SaaS is an all-inclusive restaurant — sit down, choose from the menu, and eat. You never touch the kitchen at all. Each is appropriate in different situations; the mistake is ordering à la carte when you need catering, or vice versa.
IaaS, PaaS, Serverless, and SaaS form a spectrum from maximum control (IaaS) to minimum responsibility (SaaS).
As you move toward SaaS, operational overhead drops but so does your customization ceiling.
Billing models differ: IaaS is per VM-hour, PaaS is per plan-hour, Serverless is per execution, SaaS is per seat.
Real architectures mix models: SaaS for commodity tools, Serverless for event-driven tasks, PaaS for apps, IaaS for GPU/legacy/custom-OS workloads.
The decision framework: SaaS first (commodity?), then Serverless (event-driven, spiky?), then PaaS (long-running app?), then IaaS (OS control, GPU, legacy?).
Model choice is not permanent — lift-and-shift to IaaS, then modernize to PaaS over time, is a valid phased approach.
